{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>

Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

[[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhism]] | rite= [[Rinzai school]] | deity =[[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[Rinzai school]] of [[Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[Shizuoka, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[Nara period]] as a [[Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[Sengoku period]], when the young [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[Edo]] with [[Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[Meiji period]], the tracks of the [[Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[Shimazaki Tōson]], [[Takayama Chogyū]] and [[Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>

===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
