revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

}}

During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>

==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|qid=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|qid=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>

It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite= [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] of [[:en:Japanese Zen|Japanese Zen]], located in the Okitsu neighborhood of [[:en:Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|Shimizu-ku]] ward of the city of [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka (city)]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] as a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient [[:en:Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the [[:en:Pacific Ocean|Pacific Ocean]]. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Imagawa Yoshimoto|Imagawa Yoshimoto]] took the tonsure. In the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], when the young [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] was held hostage in [[:en:Sunpu Domain|Sunpu]] by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot [[:en:Sessai Chōrō|Sessai Chōrō]]. During the invasion of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]] by the [[Takeda clan]], [[:en:Imagawa Ujizane|Imagawa Ujizane]] established his headquarters at this temple.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking [[:en:Edo|Edo]] with [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the [[:en:Joseon missions to Japan|Joseon missions to Japan]] and the delegations from the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]]. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

During the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the tracks of the [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line]] railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking [[:en:Suruga Bay|Suruga Bay]], the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], [[:en:Takayama Chogyū|Takayama Chogyū]] and [[:en:Natsume Sōseki|Natsume Sōseki]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling [[:en:Imperial Japanese Navy|Imperial Japanese Navy]] in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and [[:en:Naval strategy|fleet maneuvers]], which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a [[:en:Song dynasty|Song dynasty]] Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]] *[[:en:List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
