(Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11561385]] Wikidata name: Seiken-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q6573285|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)}} *{{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q16248984|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q6573285|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)}} *{{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q16248984|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.

The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>

During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

(Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667|1=Myōshin-ji sect}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen|1=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era|1=Hakuchi (era)}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean|1=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni|1=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu|1=Sunpu Domain}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō|1=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane|1=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan|1=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay|1=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū|1=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki|1=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers|1=Naval strategy}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay|1=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū|1=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki|1=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers|1=Naval strategy}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>

(Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667|1=Myōshin-ji sect}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen|1=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era|1=Hakuchi (era)}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean|1=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni|1=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu|1=Sunpu Domain}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō|1=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane|1=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan|1=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay|1=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū|1=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki|1=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers|1=Naval strategy}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>

The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers|1=Naval strategy}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)]][[en:Seiken-ji]][[fa:معبد سیکن]][[ja:清見寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Seikenji temple-02.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667|1=Myōshin-ji sect}}|prefecture={{ill|Shimizu-ku|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.047625|N|138.513097|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Shizuoka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiken-ji | native_name = 清見寺 | image = Seikenji temple-02.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Seiken-ji gate | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Seiken-ji | location = 418-1 Seikenji-cho, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka-shi, Shizuoka-ken | coordinates = {{coord|35|2|51.45|N|138|30|47.15|E|display=inline,title|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite= {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity =[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]] | country = {{flagicon|Japan}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | functional_status = | website ={{url|1=https://seikenji.com/}} | founded_by = unknown | year_completed = c. {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Seiken-ji'''|清見寺|}}, is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} belonging to the [[Myōshin-ji]] branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} of {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Japanese Zen|1=Japanese Zen}}, located in the Okitsu neighborhood of {{ill|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|en|Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka|WD=Q79385|lt=Shimizu-ku|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} ward of the city of {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka (city)|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its [[honzon|main image]] is a statue of [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Shaka Nyōrai]]. It is also called Kiyomi-dera.
==History== The temple claims to have been founded in the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era|1=Hakuchi (era)}} of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} as a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect temple to protect the {{nihongo|Kiyomi-seki|清見関|}}, a natural barrier on the ancient {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}} highway connecting the capital with eastern Japan, where the path of the road was a narrow ledge between cliffs and the {{ill|Pacific Ocean|en|Pacific Ocean|WD=Q98|lt=Pacific Ocean|1=Pacific Ocean}}. The temple subsequently fell into disrepair and was revived and converted to the Rinzai school by the monk {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni|1=Enni}} in 1262 AD. The temple flourished during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and was named one of the [[Five Mountain System#The ''Jissetsu''|''Jissetsu'' temples]] in 1343. It is where {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Imagawa Yoshimoto|en|Imagawa Yoshimoto|WD=Q1054305|lt=Imagawa Yoshimoto|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}} took the tonsure. In the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, when the young {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was held hostage in {{ill|Sunpu Domain|en|Sunpu Domain|WD=Q3034530|lt=Sunpu|1=Sunpu Domain}} by the [[Imagawa clan]], he was sent to Seiken-ji to be tutored by the abbot {{ill|Sessai Chōrō|en|Sessai Chōrō|WD=Q842029|lt=Sessai Chōrō|1=Sessai Chōrō}}. During the invasion of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} by the [[Takeda clan]], {{ill|Imagawa Ujizane|en|Imagawa Ujizane|WD=Q3100078|lt=Imagawa Ujizane|1=Imagawa Ujizane}} established his headquarters at this temple.
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple continued to flourish due to its connection with Tokugawa Ieyasu, and its location on the Tōkaidō highway linking {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} with {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It was selected by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] as one of the lodging spots for the {{ill|Joseon missions to Japan|en|Joseon missions to Japan|WD=Q15290174|lt=Joseon missions to Japan|1=Joseon missions to Japan}} and the delegations from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Due to these connections it was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1994.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/206953 |title=朝鮮通信使遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref> Surviving Edo period structures include the ''[[Sanmon]]'' (1651) and entrance to the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (1616).<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
During the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the tracks of the {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} railway were laid across the front of the temple, which lost much of its properties. However, due to its scenic location overlooking {{ill|Suruga Bay|en|Suruga Bay|WD=Q1201023|lt=Suruga Bay|1=Suruga Bay}}, the temple drew the attention of poets and writers, including [[:en:Shimazaki Tōson|Shimazaki Tōson]], {{ill|Takayama Chogyū|en|Takayama Chogyū|WD=Q461853|lt=Takayama Chogyū|1=Takayama Chogyū}} and {{ill|Natsume Sōseki|en|Natsume Sōseki|WD=Q180903|lt=Natsume Sōseki|1=Natsume Sōseki}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The temple was also the location of an important conference held by the leadership of the fledgling {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|WD=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} in June 1887, with the participants discussing various tactics and {{ill|Naval strategy|en|Naval strategy|WD=Q11559269|lt=fleet maneuvers|1=Naval strategy}}, which would then be tested in practice by the fleet in nearby Shimizu harbor.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Evans |first1=David C |last2=Peatty |first2=Mark |title=Kaigun: Strategy, Tactics, and Technology in the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1887-1941 |date=2012 |publisher=Naval Institute Press |isbn=978-1591142447 |pages=35–36}}</ref>
==Cultural properties==
===National Important Cultural Properties=== ====Diary of Ye Mengde==== Diary of '''Ye Mengde''' ({{zh|t=葉夢得|w='''Yeh Meng-te'''}}; 1077–1148), a {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} Chinese scholar, poet, and government minister, dated summer of 1149 AD<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156808|title=宋版石林先生尚書伝 自巻第一至第四 紹興二十九年仲夏刊記|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
===National Place of Scenic Beauty=== ==== Seiken-ji gardens==== The {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} at Seiken-ji dates from the early Edo period and contains ponds and tree arrangements. It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Seikenji-Butsuden.jpg|Butsuden File:Seikenji-Syourou.jpg|Belfry File:Seikenji temple-01.JPG|Seiken-ji from Tōkaidō Line tracks </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q6573285|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)}} *{{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|WD=Q16248984|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)|1=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Shizuoka)}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Seikenji (Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}}]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]]{{Suwa Faith}}

It became a nationally designated [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] in 1936.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/162831|title=清見寺庭園|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=}}</ref>
